learning_outcomes.description: critically examine varying systems of news media in different countries;
identify various levels of press freedom and mechanisms of media control in various countries;
understand how new technologies impact journalistic practices, especially foreign news reporting and the factors that influence the coverage of foreign events across cultures;
compare and contrast news media systems across cultures;
utilise a variety of research tools, including scholarly journals and the Internet.

assessments.category: Participation/ Student contribution, Tutorial/ Problem Set
assessments.task_description:
Participation in tutorials is an important element of learning in this course. To do well, you need to participate actively in tutorials. Rolls will be taken during tutorials.

Participation will be measured by your verbal contribution in class and your ability to undertake assigned tasks. You may miss one tutorial without penalty; please refer to the 'Deferral or extension' section below for more information.

All relevant reading resources for this course are posted in Talis Aspire to assist you to achieve your learning objectives. Students are required to read specified material intended for discussion in a particular tutorial session and be prepared to actively participate in the discussion. Important issues about the course and assessment tasks will be discussed in tutorial sessions. It is essential that you attend tutorial classes and be prepared to make valuable contributions to the discussions.

This assessment task is to be completed in-person.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: Late submission is not possible for this piece of assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.assessment_title: Critical Analysis of Issues in International News Reporting
assessments.weighting: <dd>30% 900 words maximum. The maximum word limit includes in-text references and the list of references. If you exceed the maximum word limit by 25 words, you will lose 1 mark. If you exceed by 50 words, you will lose 2 marks, etc.  </dd>
assessments.task_description:
Critical Analysis of Issues in International News Reporting (30%)

Assessment Task

You will critically analyse an international news story published in an online newspaper in the last week of August 2026 (i.e., the story must be published sometime between 24 and 31 August 2026). Any news story selected outside of this timeframe will not be accepted and will not be marked.

Your critical analysis must include research on the background and context of the news story, including some background information about the country on which the story is based.

In terms of research, your analysis should examine factors that affect journalism practice in that country. These include but are not limited to the political, cultural, social, economic, religious, technological, legal, structural, and environmental factors.

In your analysis, you should also reflect on the issues that inform international news reporting in that country, particularly the safety of journalists and their freedom to do their job without restraints. 

You should present convincing and logical arguments that support your assessment of these factors, using practical examples to validate your analysis.

Your critical analysis should start with clear justifications for your choice of the international news story that you selected. That is, why did you choose/select that particular news story?

Additionally, you should reflect on the challenges you faced while selecting the international news story, as well as the challenges you faced while analysing the story.

Your reflections on the challenges you faced while selecting the news story and the challenges you faced while analysing the story should be presented in the LESSONS LEARNED section below. See the section on the STRUCTURE of the assignment.

Your analysis must include the online newspaper’s link to the news story. This is to enable your tutor to verify the validity/authenticity of the story. 

EXCLUSIONS

Magazine news reports should not be selected and should not be analysed.

News reports about your own home country should not be selected and should not be analysed. That is, the news report must not relate to your own home country.

News reports about Australia should not be selected and should not be analysed.

These exclusions are meant to expose you to international news reporting outside of the Australian context and outside your home country environment.

GUIDELINES FOR THIS ASSESSMENT

LIMIT: 900 words (Maximum). Please stick to the stipulated maximum word limit. The word limit includes in-text references and your full list of references.

CRITERIA & MARKING: Assessment criteria are posted on Blackboard.

REFERENCING STYLE

Use the APA referencing style (7th edition). See the SS&H Library for guidelines on the APA referencing styles. APA stands for American Psychological Association.

Make sure you read the Assessment Criteria on Blackboard before undertaking this assignment.

Do not write in the first person. Do not use abbreviations. Ensure you proofread your assignment before you submit it.

STRUCTURE OF THE ASSIGNMENT

The assignment should be written like a formal essay.

Please adhere to the structure outlined below by using the following subheadings to write your assignment.

INTRODUCTION

This section should contain a couple of opening statements to flag what you will do in the assignment.

CRITICAL ANALYSIS

This is where you should present your critical analysis of the international news story you selected and respond to the questions in the assignment.

LESSONS LEARNED

This is the section where you should reflect on the assignment in general, including the challenges you faced while considering the news story to select, as well as the challenges you experienced while analysing the news story.

CONCLUSION

This is where you should wrap up your argument and make a firm and definitive/concluding statement. 

LIST OF REFERENCES

This is the section where you should list in full and in alphabetical order all the references to the literature that you cited in your assignment. Remember to use the APA referencing style (7th edition). The APA referencing style (7th edition) is available in the library. It is also in this section that you should list all the resources/works you consulted and used in your critical analysis. Only the literature, works, and resources referenced in your critical analysis should be listed in this section.

PLEASE NOTE: All times listed for all assignments are Brisbane times.

MARKS AND FEEDBACK: You will receive an email from the Course Coordinator to advise when your marks and feedback are ready in Gradebook.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
The purpose of the End-of-Semester Examination is to test your knowledge and understanding of the issues covered in this course -- in lectures, tutorials, and specified reading material. The examination will cover topics discussed in lectures, tutorials, and specific topics taken from any specified reading material.

The examination will comprise of short-answer essay questions.

PLEASE NOTE: Guidelines and advice for the End-of-Semester Examination will be presented at the final lecture in Week 12. You are encouraged to attend the lecture for advice on how you should prepare for the examination.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

Introduction to the course, the structure of lectures and tutorials, the nature of assignments and assessment criteria -- Levi Obijiofor

Powerpoint slides and audio of lectures will be available on Blackboard. Tutorial slides will also be available on Blackboard.

Week 9 Lecture:

Politics of the debate over a New World Information and Communication Order (NWICO) -- Levi Obijiofor

 

READINGS

Lecture slides and audio will be available on Blackboard.

 

Read -- Levi Obijiofor’s summary of the debate: The politics of the New World Information and Communication Order. Available on Blackboard.

 

Read -- Many Voices, One World: Towards a new more just and more efficient world information and communication order (2004). Report by the International Commission for the Study of Communication Problems. Lanham, MD: Rowman & Littlefield. Read also: Conclusions and Recommendations, pp. 253-275. LIBRARY CALL NUMBER: HM1206 .I58 2004.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 10 Tutorial:

This week's tutorial will analyse insights presented by Isabella Higgins, ABC former Europe correspondent who also previously covered Indigenous affairs for ABC News. Isabella's insights were presented in a Reuters Institute video interview titled Putting Indigenous rights on the news agenda. The video is one hour long but we will only watch a segment of the interview and discuss issues that emerged from that interview. The video link is here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mPcvPcd9oQQ. Please endeavour to watch the video and be prepared to participate in the tutorial discussion.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 12 Tutorial:

This tutorial will discuss the uneasy relationship between foreign correspondents and senior editors in overseas head offices.

 

RECOMMENDED READINGS

The following recommended readings are posted on Blackboard.

 

Read -- Gatekeeping, gatewatching, real-time feedback -- New challenges for journalism -- by Axel Bruns

Read -- Future of journalism -- In an age of digital media and economic uncertainty -- by Bob Franklin

Read -- User-generated visibility -- Secondary gatekeeping in a shared media space -- by Jane B. Singer
